notes.anchor: in the most suspicious time of Caninius
notes.note: L. Caninius Gallus, tribune of 56 BC, who had proposed that Pompey be sent to restore the Egyptian king --- a move that made Lentulus suspect Pompey of angling for the command himself. Cicero says even then Pompey privately favoured Lentulus.

notes.anchor: Ptolemy may return to his kingdom
notes.note: Ptolemy XII Auletes, exiled king of Egypt; the plan Cicero relays (from Pompey) is for Lentulus to install him at Ptolemais, take Alexandria by force, then restore him --- thus formally honouring the Senate's first decree.

A verse of the Sibylline Books was read as forbidding the king's restoration 'with a multitude' (an army). Restoring him 'without a multitude' --- the king brought back personally rather than at the head of a host --- was the device for respecting the oracle's religio.

notes.anchor: the levity and weakness of Clodius
notes.note: P. Clodius Pulcher, Cicero's great enemy who had engineered his exile; by 56 BC his political grip was loosening (Milo's gangs checking his), which Lentulus had congratulated Cicero upon.

notes.anchor: pay was decreed for Caesar, and ten legates
notes.note: Among the measures the triumvirs forced through the Senate after the Luca conference: state pay for Caesar's army and ten senatorial legati to assist him --- both extending and entrenching his Gallic command.

notes.anchor: that he should not be succeeded under the Sempronian law
notes.note: The lex Sempronia de provinciis consularibus (C. Gracchus, 123 BC) governed when consuls' provinces were assigned, allowing a governor to be replaced. Suspending it for Caesar prolonged his command in Gaul.
P. Cornelius Lentulus Spinther the younger, the recipient's son, then in his teens; Cicero commends his promise and urges that he be educated above all by imitation of his father.
